content: Cardiac rehabilitation (CR) is a comprehensive, multidisciplinary program designed to optimize cardiovascular health in patients with established CVD. Core components: (1) medically supervised exercise training, (2) cardiovascular risk factor modification, (3) psychosocial and vocational counseling, (4) patient education. Indications (Class I): post-MI, stable angina, post-PCI, post-CABG, heart valve repair/replacement, heart transplant/LVAD, stable HFrEF. Phases: Phase I (inpatient—early mobilization), Phase II (outpatient supervised—typically 36 sessions over 12 weeks, ECG monitored initially), Phase III (maintenance—supervised but less intensive), Phase IV (independent—long-term maintenance). Benefits: reduces all-cause mortality ~20-25%, CV mortality ~26%, hospital readmissions, improves functional capacity (VO2 max increases 15-30%), quality of life, psychological well-being. Mechanisms: improved endothelial function, autonomic balance, skeletal muscle metabolism, medication adherence.

Exercise prescription in CR: initial assessment includes symptom-limited exercise test to determine functional capacity, ischemic threshold, arrhythmia risk, BP response. FITT principle: Frequency (3-5 days/week aerobic, 2-3 days/week resistance), Intensity (aerobic: 40-80% heart rate reserve or RPE 11-14 on Borg scale; use heart rate at ischemic threshold minus 10 bpm if present; resistance: 30-40% 1RM upper body, 50-60% lower body initially, progress to 60-80%), Time (20-60 minutes continuous or accumulated aerobic; 1-3 sets of 10-15 repetitions resistance), Type (rhythmic large muscle groups for aerobic—walking, cycling, rowing; major muscle groups for resistance). Warm-up and cool-down essential (5-10 minutes each). Monitoring: telemetry initially for high-risk patients, BP before/during/after exercise, symptoms (angina, dyspnea, dizziness), RPE. Contraindications to exercise: unstable angina, decompensated HF, severe AS, uncontrolled arrhythmias, acute PE, myocarditis/pericarditis, resting SBP >200 or DBP >110. Progression based on tolerance, goals, comorbidities.

CR utilization and barriers: despite Class I recommendations, only 20-30% of eligible patients enroll in US; even lower globally. Barriers: patient-level (lack of awareness, transportation, time, cost, comorbidities, depression), provider-level (failure to refer, underestimate benefit), system-level (limited programs, insurance coverage gaps, geographic access). Women, elderly, racial/ethnic minorities, rural residents less likely to participate. Alternative delivery models to improve access: home-based CR (structured program with remote monitoring), telehealth/virtual CR (video conferencing, mobile apps, wearable devices), hybrid models. Meta-analyses show home-based and center-based CR have similar outcomes for functional capacity, risk factors, quality of life. Hybrid models combine initial supervised sessions with home maintenance. Mobile health technologies enable remote monitoring of HR, activity, symptoms. Insurance coverage expanding (CMS covers home-based CR since 2020). Quality metrics: enrollment rates, completion rates (≥36 sessions associated with better outcomes), achievement of risk factor goals, patient-reported outcomes.
